context_message: 2B CS looking for SWE internships (primarily backend) for summer 2026. Any advice is appreciated.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: you worked on "prediction markets" trading lol? or were you working for a sportsbook (i.e. bet pricing algorithms). just curious.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: "bachelors of math, major in cs" may be technically correct if you're in bmath cs but buries the lead. lead with the cs part for most jobs. "[bachelor of/major in] cs, minor in stats"

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: "Designed and deployed MLB trading algorithm". this phrase carries a lot of weight to me. you designed the strategy? how good is it? or did you just deploy it? also MLB like you're literally trading prop bets on MLB games right? I hope that would be clear if i knew the company name. otherwise the idea that you wrote an system to do sports betting would be pretty foreign to a lot of people (maybe offputting). i would try my best to divorce the tech stuff that you did from the product you were trading so i don't have to mention it was sports betting.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: Your second "in-game algo" bullet is vague. what latency are you measuring? tick to trade? 400ms tick to trade seems pretty bad but depends on the model/time-horizon/product. also, don't just tell me you used concurrency, caching, and a tuned http client (not sure what that one means). try to say a few words about what was concurrent or cached. something like "engineered pipeline that concurrently ingests and evaluates market data" might be one bullet and "cached xyz, acheiving 400ms latency" might be another

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: "Integrated XGBoost pricing model by compiling lightweight Treelite object and linking via cgo into Go algorithm" ngl but this sounds like 2 hours of work. you compiled a model and linked into it.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: what does bigquery have to do with the algorithm? how did we get to 80% faster predictions?

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: rest of the resume i mostly like

critiques.author: awaterloostudent
critiques.content: Thank you. This is super helpful. I updated my resume to (1) lead with CS, (2) clarify the "designed" wording to reflect my scope, (3) make the latency bullet explicit about what's being measured and frame it as optimizing for external constraints, and (4) add specific details on what was concurrent. I've also reworded the Treelite/cgo bullet to focus on the engineering/ML work.

critiques.author: awaterloostudent
critiques.content: If you have a minute, I would greatly appreciate your take on the revised bullets.

critiques.author: awaterloostudent
critiques.content: [attachments: https://cdn.discordapp.com/attachments/[PHONE_REDACTED]443332157/[PHONE_REDACTED]940032777/SWE_Resume_Updated.pdf?ex=6a52cb95&is=6a517a15&hm=7beb5987d5346b0b24db50f71bbe11fbd978ac5c9e651b4e4dfdddb623bf4923&]

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
sorry this is too late for ww cycle 1. just got around to this
I think the structure got better. i just have some minor gripes about wording:
- ML evaluation to me means evaluating the performance/accuracy of models. I would stick to saying inference
- poll-to-intent throughput should be poll-to-intent latency. you're still measuring the time it takes to perform 1 action, not how many actions you can do in a fixed time
- "standalone pre-event forecasts" is an awkward phrase. I would just drop standalone and say "pre-event forecasts". then you have room to say "by 80% by unifying..." instead of just a comma
- "Powered ML evaluation by designing" seems pointless. "Designed/built/trained XGBoost pricing model and optimized inference using Treelite and cgo" is better to me
- i think first bullet reads better as "Developed an automated trading system for event-driven markets and validated its integrity in live deployment."

applicant_profile: 1A CS looking for SWE and Robotics related internship
resume_text:
---
Experience
Stealth Startup Mountain View, CA (Remote)
Internal Engineer Jul 2025 – Aug 2025
• Implemented a dual-agent architecture: one agent streams fast replies, while a second agent performs memory/RAG
update and search asynchronously and communicates via RPC, reducing response latency from 500ms to 80ms.
• Partnered with the Letta (MemGPT) engineering team to test a real-time memory SDK for voice agents; identified
and parallelized a RAG performance bottleneck, cutting latency from 1300ms to 240ms.
• Implemented real-time avatar lip-sync and expression rendering by building an audio-to-phoneme mapping pipeline
and driving mouth/expression parameters from live audio, enabling synchronized real-time virtual chatbot avatars.
First Robotics Competition Team 2198 Scarborough, Ontario
Robot Programming Lead Sep 2021 – Apr 2025
• Enabled fully autonomous navigation + scoring for 2025 FRC through an AprilTag pose pipeline to transform
robot-relative pose to global field coordinates via rotational and translational matrices, achieving ±5 inch pose error.
• Built a vision-guided auto-intake system for the 2024 FRC game by deploying a detection model on a TPU processor
and converting bounding-box detections into robot-relative pose targets for autonomous pickup, winning Innovation in
Control (Humber College event).
• Optimized 2024 game-piece vision with an OpenCV pipeline (color thresholding + ellipse detection) while preserving
detection quality, cutting latency from 300ms to 120ms and eliminating the need for the TPU processor.
Projects
DingleBob (Custom Programming Language) | Playground Link | GitHub Link Dec 2025 – Jan 2026
• Built a custom programming language from scratch in Rust with variables, functions, closures, and block scoping;
shipped a REPL + command-line runner and multi-file imports for programs.
• Implemented reliable variable scoping and shadowing rules by adding a resolver pass that links each variable use to its
correct definition; used closures to support stateful “class and struct-like” objects.
• Improved debugging experience by implementing compiler-style error messages (exact line/column + highlighted
source), and verified outputs with snapshot-based UI test cases.
Spacey3D (Linear Algebra Visualization Tool) | Youtube Link | GitHub Link Jul 2024 – Aug 2024
• Built an interactive 3D math visualization app in JavaFX that lets users manipulate vectors, lines, planes, and linear
transformations in real time to understand 3D linear algebra concepts.
• Implemented a custom 3D renderer from scratch (camera transforms, projection, Euler angles) to draw 3D scenes onto
a 2D canvas with smooth mouse controls (rotate/pan/zoom).
• Developed the underlying linear algebra engine + input UI by building matrix/vector operations and transformation
utilities to power accurate rendering and computations from user inputs.
Faris Chatbot (VTuber AI Chatbot) | Deployment Link | GitHub Link Jul 2023 – Aug 2023
• Deployed and built an interactive AI VTuber app that lets users talk to a live animated avatar via text; implemented a
backend that manages conversations and a web UI that renders the character and chat state in real time.
• Implemented real-time avatar interactivity by integrating a Vtuber model renderer onto the frontend and driving
animation from user input with audio-based lip sync from the chatbot’s response and mouse-tracked head/eye motion.
Skills
Languages: Rust, C++, Python, Java, JavaScript
Frameworks/Tools: OpenGL, OpenCV, TensorFlow, Docker, Nix, Git, JavaFX, WPILib, React, FastAPI
Platforms & Systems: Linux, Google Coral, PhotonVision, Limelight, LiveKit, VS Code, Claude Code
Knowledge Areas: Systems programming, language design (interpreters/parsers), low-latency AI pipelines, robotics,
computer vision, linear algebra + 3D geometry
Education
University of Waterloo Waterloo, ON
Bachelor of Computer Science, B.P. Dammizio Entrance Scholarship, cGPA: 94.6 2025 - 2030

critiques.author: d2i-23
critiques.content: Purposely removed the whole name, email, and stuff header for privacy

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- internal engineer? do you not have a more standard title here?
- your bullet points are too long. longer sentences tend to be harder to parse. my heuristic is you should only have a small handful of 2-liners in your whole resume. easy first step is replace semicolons with line breaks
- From Xms to Yms is weak framing unless the numbers are really bold. prefer multiples/proportions. 150ms to 1ms sounds sick but don't make me do 1300/240 in my head to figure out how significant that was
- do you have a better name for the accelerator you used for FRC than a TPU? i read that as google TPU but ik it's not that if you had this thing deployed on a FRC bot

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
generally, i think there's a lot of fat here. i normally complain that people are too vague ("collaborated with 15 people in an AGILE team"). you include a lot of implementation details, which is an improvement, but is still sometimes irrelevant and obscures more interesting details about what you did. as an exercise, here's my inline comments on your 2 experiences (strikethrough -> redundant imo):
> Implemented a dual-agent architecture: one agent streams fast replies, while a second agent performs memory/RAG
> update and search ~~asynchronously and communicates via RPC~~, reducing response latency from 500ms to 80ms.
> • Partnered with the Letta (MemGPT) engineering team ~~to test a real-time memory SDK~~ **(maybe ok if the SDK is well-known)** for voice agents; identified
> and parallelized a RAG performance bottleneck, cutting latency from 1300ms to 240ms **(what latency is this? how is this different from the last one?)**.
> • Implemented real-time avatar lip-sync and expression rendering by building an audio-to-phoneme mapping pipeline
> ~~and driving mouth/expression parameters from live audio, enabling synchronized real-time virtual chatbot avatars.~~
> • Enabled fully autonomous navigation~~ + scoring~~ for ~~2025 ~~FRC through an AprilTag pose pipeline to transform
> robot-relative pose to global field coordinates~~ via rotational and translational matrices,~~ achieving ±5 inch pose error.
> • Built a vision-guided ~~auto-~~intake system ~~for the 2024 FRC game~~ by deploying a detection model on a TPU processor
> and converting bounding-box detections into robot-relative pose targets for autonomous pickup, winning Innovation in
> Control ~~(Humber College event)~~.
> • Optimized ~~2024 game-piece~~ vision with an OpenCV pipeline (~~color thresholding + ellipse detection~~) while preserving
> detection quality **(measured how? vague)**, cutting latency from 300ms to 120ms and eliminating the need for the TPU processor **(why was this better than TPU?)**.

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content: other stuff could still be said more concisely if i rearranged the words instead of just striking through. since this is co-op 1, it's fair to keep some fat in there to pad out your resume, but keep in mind for future

critiques.author: rayhaan
critiques.content:
- Why is your training coordinator role first? even if it's first chronologically, it's the least relevant and should go at the bottom
- I don't understand how you chose which words to bold. Why would you bold competitions but not capture the flag? Also **authored** and **enabling**? you should bold keywords that you think somebody might care about. That basically translates to technologies and stats. Maybe concepts like terminal basics is ok
- Is there anything more interesting or specific you can say about your experiences? what i get from your resume is that you worked a few QA jobs where you fulfilled what a QA person should theoretically do (automate testing) while using XYZ technologies. not much that would make you stand out in a crowd
